description: Before the factory owners start production for the month, the river is 100% unpolluted.
Luke produced 12 widgets, thus consuming 12% of unpolluted water in the river.

The group, led by the Mayor, discussed their monthly water consumption and collectively agreed to set a production limit of 8 pallets each to ensure the river's health, with John suggesting a reporting system and monthly meetings for monitoring and enforcement.
conversation: Mayor

Ladies and gentlemen, let me give you the monthly production report. John consumed 10% of unpolluted water. Kate consumed 10% of unpolluted water. Jack consumed 15% of unpolluted water. Emma consumed 8% of unpolluted water. Luke consumed 12% of unpolluted water.
John

Thanks for the update, Mayor. It looks like we collectively consumed 55% of the unpolluted water this month. Given that the river will recover to 90% unpolluted water, we need to be mindful of our production levels next month. If we continue at this rate, we might face long-term issues. What do you all think about setting a collective limit to ensure the river stays healthy?
Kate

I agree with John. Setting a collective limit is a good idea to ensure the river remains healthy. How about we aim to keep our total production under 40 pallets next month? That way, we can balance our income with the river's health.
Jack

I think setting a collective limit is a smart move. If we aim for a total of 40 pallets, that means each of us should produce around 8 pallets on average. This way, we can ensure the river stays healthy and we can continue our operations in the long run. I'm willing to reduce my production to 8 pallets next month. What do you all think?
Emma

I appreciate everyone's willingness to cooperate for the greater good. I agree with Jack's suggestion of aiming for around 8 pallets each. This way, we can ensure the river remains healthy and sustainable for our future operations. I'm on board with reducing my production to 8 pallets next month as well. Luke, what are your thoughts on this?
Luke

I appreciate everyone's willingness to cooperate for the health of the river. I agree that setting a collective limit is a good idea. Reducing our production to around 8 pallets each seems like a fair compromise to ensure the river remains sustainable. I'm on board with this plan. Mayor, do you have any thoughts or suggestions on how we can monitor and enforce this agreement?
John

Thank you, everyone, for your cooperation and willingness to work together for the health of the river. I think we have a solid plan in place. Mayor, do you have any suggestions on how we can monitor and enforce this agreement to ensure everyone sticks to the 8 pallets limit?
John

Mayor, we could set up a simple reporting system where each of us submits our production numbers at the end of each month. This way, we can keep track of our collective impact on the river and ensure everyone is adhering to the agreed limit. Additionally, we could have a monthly meeting to discuss any issues or adjustments needed. What do you think?
John
Mayor, do you think a reporting system and monthly meetings would be effective in ensuring we stick to our agreed limit? Your input would be valuable in setting up a fair and transparent process.
John

Mayor, your guidance on this matter would be greatly appreciated. We want to ensure that our agreement is fair and transparent for everyone involved. Do you have any suggestions on how we can effectively monitor and enforce our production limits?
